dialogue.utterance: Unit 3 respond to a chest pain call at RFD Station 5.
Hi, I'm Liz, an EMT with the squad. What brought you to the hospital today?
I woke up with chest pressure-rated as 8 out of 10.
Can you tell me your name and the current day of the week?
Alert and oriented to person, place, time, and events.
On a scale of 0 to 10, how would you measure the severity of this chest pain?
When did the chest pain first start today?
Are you having trouble breathing or feeling like something is stuck in your throat?

dialogue.micro_intent: check_breathing - Rate and depth of respirations
dialogue.utterance: Your breathing is steady with adequate depth and volume.
Respiratory rate is 18 breaths per minute.

dialogue.micro_intent: check_breathing - Oxygen saturation measured with pulse oximetry
dialogue.utterance: Oxygen saturation is 100%.
Pulse is 71, regular and strong.
Blood pressure is 131 over 79.
Blood glucose is at 82 mg/dL.
12-Lead EKG shows sinus rhythm, no elevation or ectopy.
Are you experiencing any other symptoms besides the chest pressure?

dialogue.micro_intent: S.A.M.P.L.E. - events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: Anything different you did before waking up with chest pressure?
Have you had any long-term medical conditions or surgeries?
Were you previously diagnosed with a stroke or COPD? Additionally, we note a history of clot and infarction.
Any allergies to food, medicines, or other substances?
What medications are you currently using?
Skin is warm, dry, no cyanosis, and no edema present.

dialogue.micro_intent: head_and_neck_exam - Pupillary reflex / Size of pupils
dialogue.utterance: Pupils are equal, round, reactive-3 mm in size.
No jugular venous distension noted.
No abnormal lung sounds; chest rises and falls evenly with no asymmetry.
Abdomen is soft, non-tender, and not distended.
No swelling or fluid accumulation in the extremities.
Pelvis is rigid, stable, and no signs of instability noted.
Placing 4-lead EKG for continuous cardiac monitoring.
Now obtaining 12-lead EKG for a more detailed view of your heart rhythm.
Per protocol, I'll now administer a 324 mg aspirin tablet orally.
Aspirin (ASA) has been administered per standing order, documented and provided with consent, following the medication administration checklist.
Now at 16 breaths per minute, regular and equal chest movement observed.
Pulse is 68 and continues to be strong.
Blood pressure is slightly lower at 122/78 and stable.
Oxygen saturation remains at 100%.
Blood glucose is reading 0-this is likely due to baseline measurement during transport phase.
Is your pain still at an 8 out of 10?
The chest pain has remained for more than 30 minutes, right?
We are following protocol 2-1 to rule out cardiac involvement.
EKG remains in sinus rhythm with no elevation or ectopy.
VCU Medical Center is the chosen destination for transport-any preference?
We'll place you in Fowler's position for comfort during transport.
Heart rhythm remains in normal sinus rhythm since transport began.
Documenting history of Infarction (CVA/clot) and COPD.
Patient also has a known history of Hepatitis C and has had multiple abscesses.
Patient is noted as having No Known Drug Allergies (NKDA).
Patient is Medicaid enrolled and has provided us with the medical record number.
You are now fully secured and comfortable for transport.
VCU Medical Center is aware of our arrival time and is preparing for your admission.
Administered 324 mg of aspirin orally, with documentation and in view of the patient.
Patient is 57-year-old male currently on Spiriva and Advair, with history of COPD and stroke.
Patient is alert throughout the care process and is documented as NKDA. MRN has been obtained and transferred with all documentation.
Field impression is Chest Pain with potential cardiac involvement and COPD exacerbation based on EKG and vitals. Glasgow Coma Scale remains full-alert and oriented (E4 V5 M6) at all times.
Patient was safely transferred by shifting from the stretcher to the hospital bed without incident.
No changes in vital signs or EKG readings were observed during transportation.
All clinical documentation, medications, and history have been handed over to the receiving RN.
Transport was initiated in "cold" status and arrival at destination was confirmed.
Motor examination was performed and no neurological deficit was observed.
